problem_statement.Title: :inherited-members option only accepts a single class name; cannot ignore multiple base classes
problem_statement.Description: The autodoc :inherited-members: option behaves as a single-class selector instead of accepting multiple class names. As a result, you cannot list multiple base classes (for example, list,set,tuple) to be ignored when deciding which inherited members to include in autoclass/automodule output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) inherited_members_option assumes a single value and returns a raw string/sentinel. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py:inherited_members_option. Current behavior: if arg in (None, True): return 'object' else: return arg. This encodes the single-name assumption and leaves options.inherited_members as a string (or whatever raw value was passed) instead of a canonical collection.

2) filter_members uses equality against the option, assuming a single-name string. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py:Documenter.filter_members (inner is_filtered_inherited_member). Current check: if cls.__name__ == self.options.inherited_members and cls != self.object: ... . This equality test only matches when the option is a single string and fails for multiple names.

3) The filter relies on ObjectMember.class_ produced by get_class_members. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py:get_class_members. get_class_members sets ObjectMember.class_ to the defining class; filter_members depends on that metadata to decide whether a member is inherited from a given class. Any change to how inherited names are represented must preserve that interaction.

4) Other call sites only test truthiness of options.inherited_members, so converting the option to a collection would preserve behavior there. Locations: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py (Documenter.get_object_members, ClassDocumenter.get_object_members) and sphinx/ext/autodoc/directive.py (option registration). A repository search found no other cls.__name__ == options.inherited_members comparisons.

5) Back-compat / sentinel handling is encoded as a single string today. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py:inherited_members_option. The sentinel 'object' (used for flag/no-arg cases) is currently returned as the string 'object'; preserving compatibility requires treating that sentinel as an element of whatever canonical collection is chosen (e.g., {'object'}).

6) End-to-end parsing shapes were not verified by the investigation. Location of potential variance: sphinx/ext/autodoc/directive.py and the directive parsing pipeline. It is unknown whether directive parsing ever supplies a list/tuple (instead of a single string) to inherited_members_option; tests for comma-separated strings, sequence inputs (list/tuple), and flag/no-arg are missing to validate all input shapes.
StepsToReproduce:
Create a module mymod.py containing classes that inherit from built-in containers and other bases, for example:
  - class A(list): pass
  - class B(A): pass
  - class C(list, CustomBase): pass

Create a Sphinx document that uses automodule:: mymod with the option :inherited-members: list,set,tuple (a comma-separated list) or with :inherited-members: ['list', 'tuple'] if passing a sequence is supported.
Build the docs (autodoc enabled) and inspect the generated class member lists for A, B, and C.
Alternatively, use autoclass:: mymod.C with :inherited-members: list and observe members inherited from list.
Observe whether members inherited from the listed base classes (e.g., list, set, tuple) are excluded from the generated output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When :inherited-members: names multiple classes (comma-separated string or provided as a list/tuple), autodoc should treat those names as a collection and ignore inherited members defined on any of the listed base classes for the documented classes. The previous flag/no-argument behavior (implicit 'object' sentinel) should continue to work.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Specifying multiple class names in :inherited-members: does not exclude members from all listed bases. The option is treated as a single value, so comma-separated names are not interpreted as multiple class names; members inherited from the intended bases (for example list, set, tuple) remain documented.

+1: Acceptable change.
>A class inherits from multiple other classes. I want to document members from some of the base classes but ignore some of the base classes

For example, there is a class that inherits multiple base classes:
```
class MyClass(Parent1, Parent2, Parent3, ...):
    pass
```
and

```
.. autoclass:: example.MyClass
   :inherited-members: Parent2
```

How should the new `:inherited-members:` work? Do you mean that the member of Parent2 are ignored and the Parent1's and Parent3's are documented? And how about the methods of the super classes of `Parent1`?

Note: The current behavior is ignoring Parent2, Parent3, and the super classes of them (including Parent1's also). In python words, the classes after `Parent2` in MRO list are all ignored.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints = "description" produces duplicated *args/**kwargs entries in autodoc output
problem_statement.Description: When using autodoc with autodoc_typehints = "description", documented varargs and kwargs (e.g. *args, **kwargs) are rendered twice and incompletely in the generated documentation. The duplication occurs because docstring-parsed parameter names that include leading '*' do not match the annotation keys produced from the function signature, causing the typehint-merging logic to re-insert parameter and type fields.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) record_typehints() records annotations using inspect.signature parameter names (param.name) as keys (e.g. 'args', 'kwargs') — these keys do not include leading '*' characters. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:record_typehints (signature iteration and annotation[param.name] assignment).

2) modify_field_list() parses docstring field names and uses the parsed name tokens verbatim as keys in its arguments map (for example, using parts[1] or ' '.join(parts[2:]) directly). Docstrings that document varargs/kwargs with leading '*' therefore produce keys like '*args'/'**kwargs' in this map, which do not match the annotation keys. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:modify_field_list.

3) augment_descriptions_with_types() likewise extracts parameter names from parsed docstring fields and adds them to its has_description and has_type sets using the parsed tokens without removing leading '*' characters. As a result, documented varargs/kwargs are not recognized as matching the annotation keys and the merging logic may inject duplicate 'type'/'param' fields. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:augment_descriptions_with_types.

4) merge_typehints() coordinates the flow: it supplies the annotations gathered by record_typehints() (non-starred parameter names) to modify_field_list() or augment_descriptions_with_types(). Because the recorded annotation keys and the parsed docstring keys use different token forms (non-starred vs. possibly-starred), the consumer code can fail to detect that a parameter is already described and may add duplicate entries. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:merge_typehints.

5) Investigation gap (unverified): the change in behavior arises from mismatched token forms (starred vs non-starred) between signature-produced keys and docstring-parsed keys. It was not verified whether any third-party extension or external code expects literal '*' in parsed field-name keys; such consumers, if present, could be affected by any change to how parsed names are normalized. This dependency remains unverified and should be considered when evaluating fixes.
StepsToReproduce: Create a module with a class whose __init__ has signature (self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) and docstring documenting :param x:, :param *args:, and :param **kwargs: (as in the provided example).
Enable the autodoc extension and set autodoc_typehints = "description" in conf.py.
Use autodoc to include the class in Sphinx documentation (e.g. automodule/autoclass or autodoc directives).
Build the Sphinx documentation and inspect the generated output for the class and its __init__.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated documentation shows a single set of parameter entries for x, *args, and **kwargs with their types and full docstring descriptions (varargs and kwargs appear once, with their types shown in the parameter list and their descriptions under the function).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated documentation shows duplicated entries for varargs and kwargs: the parameter list includes *args and **kwargs (typed) and then separate/incomplete entries for *args/**kwargs appear again under the function/method details. Descriptions and types for varargs/kwargs may appear twice or be missing from one of the duplicate entries.

I noticed this docstring causes warnings because `*` and `**` are considered as mark-up symbols:

```
    def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
        """Init docstring.

        :param x: Some integer
        :param *args: Some integer
        :param **kwargs: Some integer
        """
```

Here are warnings:
```
/Users/tkomiya/work/tmp/doc/example.py:docstring of example.ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:6: WARNING: Inline emphasis start-string without end-string.
/Users/tkomiya/work/tmp/doc/example.py:docstring of example.ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:7: WARNING: Inline strong start-string without end-string.
```

It will work fine if we escape `*` character like the following. But it's not officially recommended way, I believe.

```
    def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
        """Init docstring.

        :param x: Some integer
        :param \*args: Some integer
        :param \*\*kwargs: Some integer
        """
```

I'm not sure this feature is really needed?

problem_statement.Title: Setting rst_prolog causes top-level headings that start with a role/domain (e.g. :mod:`...`) to be dropped or not included in toctree
problem_statement.Description: When config.rst_prolog is set, documents whose top-level title is written using a role/domain inline markup (for example ":mod:`mypkg`") are not recognized as section titles and therefore do not render correctly or appear in the toctree. The issue occurs only when rst_prolog is present and the heading uses role/inline-role syntax as the document's first heading line.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Overly-permissive docinfo detection regex in sphinx/util/rst.py.
   - Location: sphinx/util/rst.py (module variable docinfo_re defined at line 28).
   - Problematic assumption: any line beginning with ":<word>:" is treated as a docinfo metadata field.
   - Code evidence: docinfo_re = re.compile(':\\w+:.*?') (sphinx/util/rst.py:28). This pattern matches role/inline-role constructs like ":mod:`name`" because it only requires a colon, word characters, a colon, then any characters.
   - Correct relationship (diagnostic): docinfo fields in reST are metadata of the form ":field: value" (i.e. a second colon followed by whitespace and the field value). Docinfo detection should require that whitespace (or an explicit value delimiter) follows the second colon so role/inline-role forms (which typically have no whitespace after the second colon, e.g. ":mod:`name`") are not misclassified.

2) prepend_prolog assumes docinfo_re matches only metadata and uses that to choose where to insert the prolog.
   - Location: sphinx/util/rst.py::prepend_prolog (function beginning at line 78).
   - Problematic assumption: prepend_prolog treats any leading lines matched by docinfo_re as docinfo metadata and inserts a blank ('<generated>') after them and places the prolog after that position. Code evidence: prepend_prolog iterates over content and increments pos while docinfo_re.match(line) is true, then inserts generated blanks and prolog at that position (sphinx/util/rst.py:78–97).
   - Why this matters (diagnostic statement): if a role/inline-role line is misclassified as docinfo, the insertion of blank/prolog lines will separate the role line from the section underline (or otherwise alter the document structure), preventing docutils from recognizing the title.

3) The parse entry point applies prepend_prolog before docutils parsing, so misclassification affects title detection and toctree extraction.
   - Location: sphinx/parsers.py::RSTParser.decorate (line 83–86) which calls prepend_prolog(content, self.config.rst_prolog) prior to running the state machine.
   - Problematic assumption: calling prepend_prolog here is safe only if docinfo detection does not match role/inline-role lines. Because prepend_prolog is invoked at parse-time, any misclassification propagates into docutils parsing and downstream steps that rely on titles.

4) Lack of a regression test for role-lined titles.
   - Location: tests/test_util_rst.py::test_prepend_prolog (tests/test_util_rst.py lines 30–48).
   - Problematic assumption: existing tests verify prolog handling with conventional docinfo lines (e.g. ':title: ...' which include whitespace after the second colon) but do not include a negative test asserting that inline-role syntax such as ':mod:`x`' should not be treated as docinfo. This omission allowed the overly-permissive detection to go unchecked in unit tests.
StepsToReproduce: sphinx-quickstart --no-sep --project mypackage --author me -v 0.1.0 --release 0.1.0 --language en docs

problem_statement.Title: autosummary: module template 'members' includes imported members despite autosummary_imported_members=False
problem_statement.Description: When generating autosummary module pages, the template variable `members` contains imported names (for example, the imported module 'os') even when conf.py sets autosummary_imported_members = False. As a result, generated module autosummary blocks list imported modules/objects in the template variable `members`.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) generate_autosummary_content unconditionally assigns template variable members from dir(obj) for modules and classes.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::generate_autosummary_content (module case: ns['members'] = dir(obj); class case: ns['members'] = dir(obj]). The code sets ns['members'] directly from dir(obj) without applying the imported_members configuration.

2) The autosummary imported-members configuration is read and passed into the generator but not applied to the members variable.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py::process_generate_options reads app.config.autosummary_imported_members and calls generate_autosummary_docs(..., imported_members=...). That imported_members argument is forwarded into generate_autosummary_content, but generate_autosummary_content does not use it when populating ns['members'].

3) Helper functions apply a different (filtered) membership heuristic than ns['members'].
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_members. get_members uses the imported flag and the condition
         if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__
     to decide whether to include a candidate member. This shows the intended membership relationship when imported_members is False; however, ns['members'] (set from dir(obj)) does not reflect this filtered view.

4) Module attribute detection depends on the unfiltered members list and therefore is affected by the discrepancy.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_module_attrs consumes the ns['members'] value to select documented module attributes (ModuleAnalyzer.find_attr_docs results are matched against members). An unfiltered ns['members'] can therefore change which attributes are considered documented or appear in templates.

5) The unconditional dir(obj) population pattern appears in both module and class branches of generate_autosummary_content.
   - Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py (module branch lines setting ns['members'] = dir(obj); class branch also sets ns['members'] = dir(obj)), indicating the same root-cause pattern in multiple locations.

6) Existing tests exercise the imported-members behavior in some cases but do not assert the module-template ns['members'] semantics for the False case.
   - Location: tests/test_ext_autosummary.py::test_autosummary_imported_members and tests/roots/test-ext-autosummary-imported_members/conf.py (the testroot sets autosummary_imported_members = True). There is no test asserting that the generated module template variable members excludes imported modules when autosummary_imported_members is False.
StepsToReproduce: Create _templates/autosummary/module.rst with a module autosummary template that iterates over members and prints each item.

Create index.rst that references example in an autosummary toctree.

Build the documentation with Sphinx (e.g. make html or sphinx-build) and inspect generated/example.rst; the generated module autosummary will list imported members such as 'os' in the autosummary block.

problem_statement.Title: Napoleon emits escaped trailing underscore in directive headers (visible backslash in HTML)
problem_statement.Description: When using sphinx.ext.napoleon, attribute and method names that end with a trailing underscore (for example `hello_`) are emitted into directive headers as `hello\_`, producing a visible backslash in the generated HTML.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs unconditionally replaces a trailing underscore with an escaped form. The implementation contains: if name.endswith('_'): name = name[:-1] + r'\_' and also returns escaped leading-star forms (r'\*', r'\**'). This means parsed names for fields may contain literal backslash characters.

2) Both GoogleDocstring and NumpyDocstring parse field names via their _consume_field implementations and call _escape_args_and_kwargs on the parsed name. Evidence: GoogleDocstring._consume_field calls _escape_args_and_kwargs(_name) (docstring.py) and NumpyDocstring._consume_field does the same in the subclass. As a result, the escaped names produced by _escape_args_and_kwargs propagate in the parsed field tuples returned to later formatting/emission code.

3) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section inserts the parsed (already-escaped) name directly into an attribute directive header in one code path: lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name). When _name contains r'\_' this backslash is placed verbatim into the directive argument.

4) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section likewise inserts the parsed name directly into a method directive header: lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name). An escaped trailing underscore in _name therefore appears verbatim in the directive argument.

5) Inline-field formatters and tests demonstrate that some RST contexts expect the escaped underscore. For example, when napoleon_use_ivar is True, attribute output is emitted as ':ivar <name>:' and the test test_underscore_in_attribute expects ':ivar arg\\_:' and ':vartype arg\\_:' in the produced RST. Thus the escaping behavior is required for inline-field contexts, and the same escaped string is both correct in some places and problematic when inserted into directive headers.

Affected locations (equal weight, listed):
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs (escape implementation)
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._consume_field (caller that applies the escape)
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::NumpyDocstring._consume_field (caller that applies the escape)
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section (emits '.. attribute:: <name>')
- sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section (emits '.. method:: <name>')
- tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py::test_underscore_in_attribute (documents expected escaped underscore in inline-field output)

Note: This diagnosis identifies the mismatch between a global escaping policy (applied at parse time) and contexts where that escaped text is later used verbatim in directive headers, producing visible backslashes in rendered HTML. It does not prescribe a remediation.

problem_statement.Title: HTML index shows two 'Symbols' sections/links when index entries start with mixed ASCII and non‑ASCII symbol characters
problem_statement.Description: When index entries use leading symbol characters with heterogeneous Unicode codepoints (for example '@' U+0040, '£' U+00A3, '←' U+2190), the generated HTML index can contain two separate 'Symbols' sections/links instead of one. Both links appear in the index navigation but point to the same anchor/first section.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect sentinel assumption in sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py::IndexEntries.create_index (top-level sorting keyfunc). The code prefixes alphabetic/underscore keys with chr(127):
   if lckey[0:1].isalpha() or lckey.startswith('_'):
       lckey = chr(127) + lckey
   This encodes the assumption that chr(127) sorts after symbol characters. That assumption is false for Unicode symbols with codepoints > 127 (for example '£' U+00A3, '←' U+2190); such symbols will not be grouped together with ASCII symbols, producing non-contiguous symbol entries.

2) Same incorrect sentinel logic in the subentry sorting function within the same method (keyfunc2). The code uses the same chr(127) prefix for subentry keys:
   if key[0:1].isalpha() or key.startswith('_'):
       key = chr(127) + key
   Both the top-level and subentry key functions rely on the same sentinel property; because chr(127) does not satisfy the intended ordering across all Unicode codepoints, symbol-leading entries may be ordered inconsistently between top-level keys and subentries.

3) Grouping and labeling produce multiple display groups with the same label when symbol entries are non-contiguous. The grouping function maps non-letter-leading entries to the display key _('Symbols') (i.e. "Symbols") and groupby(newlist, keyfunc3) combines only consecutive items. When symbol-leading entries are split (some before letters, some after), create_index returns multiple groups that each have the display key 'Symbols'.

4) Downstream consumer manifests duplicate headings/links. StandaloneHTMLBuilder.write_genindex consumes create_index's output and (when split index is used) builds per-key pages and sidebar links using the display key string (handle_page('genindex-' + key) and the template's pathto('genindex-' + key)). Multiple groups with the identical display key therefore produce multiple identical links/headings that point to the same page/anchor.

5) Tests do not cover the non-ASCII symbol sorting case. Existing unit tests assert symbol-first behavior for ASCII-symbol cases (e.g. '&-symbol', '9-symbol') but do not include non-ASCII symbol characters such as U+00A3 or U+2190; thus the behavior described was not detected by the current tests.
StepsToReproduce: Create Sphinx index entries that begin with the characters '@' (U+0040), '£' (U+00A3), and '←' (U+2190) in the documentation source so they appear in the general index.
Build the HTML documentation (e.g., make html).
Open the generated index page (genindex.html) and inspect the index links/headings.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: There is a single 'Symbols' section in the HTML index containing all symbol-leading entries, and a single 'Symbols' link in the index navigation that points to that section.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The HTML index shows two 'Symbols' sections/links: one for some symbol entries (e.g. '@') placed before the letter blocks and another for other symbol entries (e.g. '£', '←') placed after the letters. Both links appear in the index but point to the same anchor/first section.

problem_statement.Title: Man page builder writes flat man pages incompatible with MANPATH
problem_statement.Description: The Sphinx man-page builder currently writes every generated man page as a flat file named <name>.<section> directly into the build output directory (outdir), rather than placing pages in per-section subdirectories (man1/, man3/, ...). This layout is incompatible with the Unix man program's MANPATH/section-directory lookup.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/builders/manpage.py:ManualPageBuilder.write encodes a flat-layout assumption. The implementation constructs the target filename and writes it into the build output directory without creating section subdirectories. Evidence: the code constructs
    targetname = '%s.%s' % (name, section)
and opens the destination with
    destination_path = path.join(self.outdir, targetname)
(sphinx/builders/manpage.py lines showing targetname and destination). This places files as <outdir>/<name>.<section>.

2) tests/test_build_manpage.py encodes and verifies the same flat-layout assumption in the test suite. Tests assert the existence and read the contents of files at app.outdir / '<name>.<section>' (for example 'sphinxtests.1' and 'python.1'), which documents and preserves the current layout in tests (tests/test_build_manpage.py lines showing these assertions).

3) sphinx/writers/manpage.py (ManualPageWriter/ManualPageTranslator) do not determine filesystem layout; they only set page metadata (including manual_section used in the .TH header). Evidence: the translator sets self._docinfo['manual_section'] = self.settings.section and uses it in the .TH header, but contains no path or filesystem-writing logic (sphinx/writers/manpage.py lines showing manual_section and header template). This shows the builder is the single component that decides where man page files are placed.

4) The codebase contains directory-creation utilities and examples of builders creating per-output directories elsewhere (e.g., sphinx.util.osutil.ensuredir and its use in other builders such as texinfo). Evidence: ensuredir is defined in sphinx/util/osutil.py and other builders use ensuredir to create output directories. The absence of analogous directory-creation logic in ManualPageBuilder.write is part of the root cause of the flat layout.
StepsToReproduce: Create or use a Sphinx project that builds man pages.
Run the man builder: sphinx-build -b man <sourcedir> <outdir> (or use `make man`).
List the build output directory: ls <outdir> and ls <outdir>/man1 (or equivalent).
Observe that generated files like <name>.<section> exist directly under <outdir> and that <outdir>/man1 (and other manN dirs) do not exist.
Optionally, attempt to use man with the build directory on MANPATH: MANPATH=<outdir> man <name> and observe man does not find the page because it expects section subdirectories.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible: Generated man pages are discoverable by the standard man/MANPATH lookup. The build output places each page in a section subdirectory, for example <outdir>/man1/<name>.1, <outdir>/man3/<name>.3, etc., so tools that search MANPATH can find them without additional packaging steps.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: All generated man pages are written directly into the build output directory as files named <name>.<section> (for example sphinxtests.1). No per-section directories (man1/, man3/, ...) are created, so man/MANPATH cannot find the pages when pointed at the build directory.

hints_text:
I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory. The build directory is not an appropriate directory to manage man pages. So no section directory is needed, AFAIK. I don't know why do you want to set `MANPATH` to the output directory. To check the output, you can give the path to the man file for man command like `man _build/man/sphinx-build.1`. Please let me know your purpose in detail.
From a [separate github thread](https://github.com/flux-framework/flux-core/pull/3033#issuecomment-662515605) that describes the specific use case in some more detail:
> When run in a builddir, `src/cmd/flux` sets `MANPATH` such that `man flux` will display the current builddir version of `flux.1`. This is done so that documentation matches the version of Flux being run.

Essentially, we are trying to make running in-tree look as similar to running an installed version as possible.

---

> I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory.

On `make install`, we do have the automake setup to copy the manpages to `$prefix/man/man1`, `$prefix/man/man3`, etc.  This did require some extra work though, since each source file and its destination has to be explicitly enumerated in the automake file.  If the man pages were built into their respective sections, a recursive copy would work too.  Not a huge deal, but just another factor I wanted to bring up.
Understandable. +1 to change the structure of output directory. As commented, it causes a breaking change for users. So I propose you to add a configuration `man_make_section_directory = (True | False)` for migration. During 3.x, it defaults to False, and it will default to True on 4.0 release. What do you think?

>I’m happy to take a crack at implementing a fix, though this change in behavior may break some people who expect everything to appear in a man/ directory.

It would be very nice if you send us a PR :-)

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints ignored for overloaded callables (overload signatures still show annotations)
problem_statement.Description: When autodoc_typehints is set to hide or move type annotations (for example 'none' or 'description'), overloaded callables still render their annotations in the displayed overload signatures. The configuration is honored for non-overloaded signatures but not for the overload-rendering code paths.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) In the overloaded-signature branches for functions, methods, and classes, the code iterates ModuleAnalyzer.overloads and calls stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) without applying the autodoc_typehints-based default to kwargs (i.e. not setting show_annotation based on self.env.config.autodoc_typehints). Locations: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: FunctionDocumenter.format_signature (overload branch) — call to stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs); MethodDocumenter.format_signature (overload branch) — call to stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs); ClassDocumenter.format_signature (overload branch) — call to stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs).

2) stringify_signature has defaults that show annotations unless callers explicitly request otherwise: def stringify_signature(sig: inspect.Signature, show_annotation: bool = True, show_return_annotation: bool = True) -> str. Because overloaded-signature code paths do not set show_annotation, stringify_signature's default (show_annotation=True) is used and parameter/return annotations appear. Location: sphinx/util/inspect.py: stringify_signature (show_annotation default True).

3) Non-overloaded formatting paths already apply autodoc_typehints correctly by setting kwargs.setdefault('show_annotation', False) when self.env.config.autodoc_typehints is 'none' or 'description'. Those checks are present in FunctionDocumenter.format_args, MethodDocumenter.format_args, and ClassDocumenter.format_args, creating an inconsistency between non-overloaded and overloaded rendering. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: FunctionDocumenter.format_args; MethodDocumenter.format_args; ClassDocumenter.format_args.

4) The typehints extension records annotations from the implementation object (the actual callable) by calling inspect.signature(obj) during the 'autodoc-process-signature' event. It does not extract annotations from the analyzer-produced overload Signature objects. Therefore, if overload annotations are present only in the parsed overload signatures (and not on the implementation object), the typehints extension will not record them for 'description' mode. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py: record_typehints (uses inspect.signature(obj)).
StepsToReproduce: In conf.py set: autodoc_typehints = 'none' (or 'description').
Create a module with overloaded functions, e.g. use typing.overload to define multiple annotated stubs and a concrete implementation without annotations.
In index.rst include the module with autodoc directives, e.g.:
.. automodule:: example
:members:
:undoc-members:

Build the documentation (e.g. sphinx-build) and inspect the generated docs for the overloaded callable signatures. Overload signatures will display parameter/return annotations despite autodoc_typehints being set to hide or move typehints.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When autodoc_typehints is 'none', type annotations should not appear in the rendered signatures for overloaded callables. When autodoc_typehints is 'description', annotations should be absent from signatures and the type information should be available in the parameter/return descriptions in the documentation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Overloaded callable signatures display type annotations in the generated documentation despite autodoc_typehints being set to 'none' or 'description'. Non-overloaded signatures respect the configuration while overloaded-rendering code paths do not.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_type_aliases not applied to module-level variables and class attributes
problem_statement.Description: The autodoc_type_aliases mapping in conf.py is ignored for module-level variables and class attributes, so forward-reference type names (e.g. 'String') remain unresolved in generated documentation for variables/attributes.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) DataDocumenter (module-level variables) does not supply the configured alias mapping when evaluating annotations: In sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, DataDocumenter.add_directive_header calls get_type_hints(self.parent) without passing the configured autodoc_type_aliases mapping (see line ~1705). This encodes the incorrect assumption that evaluating annotations for module-level data requires no external type alias mapping; as a result, forward-reference names are not resolved using the user's autodoc_type_aliases.

2) AttributeDocumenter (class attributes) does not supply the configured alias mapping when evaluating annotations: In sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, AttributeDocumenter.add_directive_header calls get_type_hints(self.parent) without passing the configured autodoc_type_aliases mapping (see line ~2096). This is the same incorrect assumption applied to class attributes, and it causes class-attribute forward refs to remain unresolved.

3) Other documenters demonstrate the intended relationship: Several other documenters and utilities in the same codebase pass the configured mapping when resolving annotations/signatures (e.g. inspect.signature(..., type_aliases=self.config.autodoc_type_aliases) and evaluate_signature(..., self.config.autodoc_type_aliases); sphinx.util.inspect.signature calls typing.get_type_hints(subject, None, type_aliases)). These locations show the expected pattern that annotation-evaluation paths should receive the autodoc_type_aliases mapping to resolve typedef aliases.

4) String-annotation / analyzer fallback is not post-processed with aliases: In both DataDocumenter and AttributeDocumenter, when get_type_hints raises NameError the code falls back to raw __annotations__ or to analyzer.annotations (string form) and uses those values verbatim in the generated :type: output (see the analyzer.annotations checks in the same methods). Those fallback code paths do not apply the autodoc_type_aliases mapping to string-based annotations.

Collectively, the root cause is that the variable/attribute annotation-resolution paths do not receive or apply the configured autodoc_type_aliases mapping (while other annotation/signature resolution paths do), and the fallback string-annotation paths are not post-processed to apply alias mappings.
StepsToReproduce:
Create example.py with the following contents:

from __future__ import annotations

#: blah blah blah
var: String


class MyString:
    "mystring"

    #: blah blah blah
    var: String

Create index.rst containing:

.. automodule:: example
   :members:
   :undoc-members:
Build the docs with Sphinx (e.g. run sphinx-build -b html . _build/html) and inspect the generated documentation for example.var and example.MyString.var.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Documentation for module-level variables and class attributes should show their types resolved according to autodoc_type_aliases (forward-reference name 'String' should be resolved to example.MyString as specified in the mapping).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In the generated documentation, module variable example.var and class attribute example.MyString.var display the unresolved forward-reference name ('String') instead of the mapped type (example.MyString). Function/method signatures that already pass type_aliases are not affected.

problem_statement.Title: Sphinx 3.3: "no number is assigned for table:" warnings from mismatched env.toc_fignumbers shape in singlefile builds
problem_statement.Description: After upgrading to Sphinx 3.3, building documentation with single-file/aggregated builders (singlehtml and similar aggregated doctree builds) produces repeated "WARNING: no number is assigned for table:" messages. The warnings are triggered when numref/figure/section/displaymath resolution fails to find assigned numbers during cross-reference resolution in single-file/aggregated builds.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. StandardDomain.get_fignumber (sphinx/domains/std.py::StandardDomain.get_fignumber) assumes env.toc_secnumbers and env.toc_fignumbers are keyed by each document's docname and looks up numbers as env.toc_secnumbers[docname] and env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][id]. When the per-doc key is missing the method raises ValueError and its caller logs the "no number is assigned for ..." warning. (See std.py lines 1061-1071 and 1073-1079; warning logged in _resolve_numref_xref at lines 850-856.)

2. SingleFileHTMLBuilder.assemble_toc_fignumbers (sphinx/builders/singlehtml.py::SingleFileHTMLBuilder.assemble_toc_fignumbers) produces a different mapping shape for single-file builds: it encodes document+figtype into alias strings of the form "%s/%s" % (docname, figtype) and returns the mapping under a single top-level key (self.config.master_doc). Concretely, assemble_toc_fignumbers constructs new_fignumbers["docname/figtype"][id] = fignum and returns { master_doc: new_fignumbers } (singlehtml.py lines 116-123). This shape differs from the canonical per-doc mapping expected by consumers.

3. MathDomain.resolve_xref (sphinx/domains/math.py::MathDomain.resolve_xref) likewise indexes env.toc_fignumbers by docname and uses env.toc_fignumbers[docname]['displaymath'].get(node_id, ()) to obtain equation/displaymath numbers; if docname is not a top-level key it treats the numbers as missing (math.py lines 109-113).

4. TocTreeCollector.assign_figure_numbers (sphinx/environment/collectors/toctree.py::TocTreeCollector.assign_figure_numbers) is the canonical writer that populates the per-doc mapping env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][id] in normal multi-page builds (toctree.py lines 218-259). The single-file alias mapping produced by SingleFileHTMLBuilder is therefore incompatible with consumers that only try the per-doc docname key.

5. The same aliasing pattern applies to section numbers in SingleFileHTMLBuilder.assemble_toc_secnumbers (singlehtml.py lines 96-102); StandardDomain.section number lookup similarly expects env.toc_secnumbers[docname] and can be affected by the same shape mismatch.

6. Repository-wide usage: direct indexing of env.toc_fignumbers by docname is present in sphinx/domains/std.py and sphinx/domains/math.py; other consumers (e.g., the regular HTML builder) use env.toc_fignumbers.get(docname, {}) safely (sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py line 591). The set of direct consumers found in the codebase aligns with the locations above.
StepsToReproduce: Upgrade a Sphinx project to version 3.3.

Build the documentation with the single-file HTML builder (singlehtml) or another build path that aggregates doctrees into a single document (the same aggregation approach is used by latex/aggregated-doctree paths).
Observe repeated warnings such as: "WARNING: no number is assigned for table:" and missing numbers in numref references in the generated output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Table/figure/section/displaymath references (numref/xref) are resolved and display their assigned numbers during single-file/aggregated builds; no spurious "no number is assigned for ..." warnings should appear when numbering was assigned by the environment collector.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Build output contains repeated warnings such as "WARNING: no number is assigned for table:" and other references (figures, sections, displaymath) appear without assigned numbers in single-file/aggregated builds, despite numbering being assigned in the environment collector.

hints_text: I digged into this a little bit more and it seems like the `id` of the table isn't properly making it into `env.toc_fignumbers`. If I set `:name: mylabel`, regardless the I see something like this in `env.toc_fignumbers` ``` 'pagename': {'table': {'id3': (1,)}, ``` So it seems like `id3` is being used for the table id instead of `mylabel` @choldgraf I suspect it's related to this: https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/commit/66dda1fc50249e9da62e79380251d8795b8e36df. Oooohhh good find! 👏👏👏 Confirmed that this was the issue - we had been referencing Tables that didn't have a title with `numref`, and this bugfix (I guess it was a bugfix?) caused us to start raising errors. Perhaps this restriction about tables needing a title could be documented more clearly? The `numfig` option has been described as follows. >If true, figures, tables and code-blocks are automatically numbered if they have a caption. https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/configuration.html#confval-numfig It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. > It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? I could understand such restrictions for Jupyter Book but it doesn't make a lot of sense for Sphinx IMO. I think Sphinx should allow users to have enumerable nodes with no caption. What do you think @choldgraf? >This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? Yes. Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. I don't know how many people want to assign numbers to non-captioned items. But this is the first feature request, AFAIK. I think my take is that I don't think it is super useful to be able to have numbered references for things that don't have titles/captions. However, it also didn't feel like it *shouldn't* be possible, and so I assumed that it was possible (and thus ran into what I thought was a bug). I think it would be more helpful to surface a more informative warning like "You attempted to add a numbered reference to a Table without a title, add a title for this to work." (or, surface this gotcha in the documentation more obviously like with a `warning` or `note` directive?) @tk0miya @choldgraf both make good points for restricting `figure` and `table` directives with no caption. My issue is that this is done at the enumerable node which implies that **all** enumerable nodes with no title/caption are skipped - not just `figure` and `table`. > Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. Just to clarify, `numfig` feature has - prior to v3.3.0 - supported uncaptioned tables but it did not display the caption. The user was able to reference the table using `numref` role (see example below). In the event that the user tried to reference the caption (aka `name` placeholder), Sphinx threw a warning indicating that there was no caption. This solution seemed sensible to me because it allowed other extensions to utilize enumerable nodes regardless of caption/no caption restriction. My main motivation for wanting to revert back or restrict the bugfix to tables and figures is because both the extensions I've worked on depend on the utilization of enumerable nodes regardless of captions/no captions. I think it wouldn't be too difficult to add the information to `env.toc_fignumbers` but I wanted to make a case before I addressed this in [sphinx-proof](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-proof) and [sphinx-exercise](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-exercise). **Example** Sphinx Version - v3.2.1 ````md ```{list-table} :header-rows: 1 :name: table1 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table1`. ```{list-table} Caption here :header-rows: 1 :name: table2 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table2`. ```` So it sounds like the `tl;dr` from @najuzilu is that in other extensions, she is *using* the fact that you can reference non-captioned elements with a number, and that Sphinx now removing this ability is breaking those extensions. Is that right? That's correct @choldgraf This is a screenshot of the PDF that is generated from @najuzilu 's example with v3.2.1. As you see, it does not work correctly in LaTeX output. I'd not like to support assigning numbers to no captioned items until fixed this (if somebody needs it).

problem_statement.Title: Sphinx 3.2 rejects option:: tokens containing '[' or '=' (e.g. "[enable=]PATTERN")
problem_statement.Description: When building documentation with Sphinx 3.2, tokens in the option:: directive that include characters like '[' or '=' (for example "[enable=]PATTERN") fail to be parsed by the standard domain. Sphinx emits a "Malformed option description" warning for such tokens and they are not indexed or resolvable; earlier Sphinx versions accepted these tokens without complaint.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The option parser's regular expression is overly restrictive (location: sphinx/domains/std.py). option_desc_re is defined as:
   option_desc_re = re.compile(r'((?:/|--|-|\+)?[^\s=[]+)(=?\s*.*)')
   The first capture group [^\s=[]+ explicitly forbids '[' and '=' inside the option-name portion, so tokens containing those characters (for example "[enable=]PATTERN") do not match the regex and therefore are not recognized as valid option names.

2) Cmdoption.handle_signature treats any token that does not match option_desc_re as malformed and skips it (location: sphinx/domains/std.py, class Cmdoption, method handle_signature). The code calls option_desc_re.match(potential_option) and, if no match, logs the warning "Malformed option description %r, should look like ..." and continues without adding the token to signode['allnames']. This encodes the assumption that a non-match is necessarily a malformed option and must not be indexed.

3) If every token in a directive's signature fails to match, handle_signature raises ValueError (location: sphinx/domains/std.py, Cmdoption.handle_signature, lines after the token loop). This is a direct consequence of the skip-all-nonmatches behavior and can escalate the build failure beyond the emitted warnings.

4) The warning text itself is prescriptive about a narrow set of accepted forms (location: sphinx/domains/std.py, Cmdoption.handle_signature). The literal warning string lists only forms like "opt", "-opt args", "--opt args", "/opt args" and "+opt args", revealing the restricted expectation coded into the parser and user-facing message.

5) Downstream indexing and resolution rely on the canonical names produced by handle_signature (locations: Cmdoption.add_target_and_index; StandardDomain.add_program_option; StandardDomain._resolve_option_xref in sphinx/domains/std.py). Because non-matching tokens are not added to signode['allnames'], add_program_option is never called for them and they are not stored in StandardDomain.progoptions, so option cross-references and program-option indexing cannot resolve those tokens.

6) Compatibility impact / canonical-key considerations: the canonical key stored for a program option is exactly the optname produced by the regex parsing (optname = m.groups()[0]). Changes in what the parser accepts or how it splits name vs args will change those canonical keys. Tokens that were previously accepted but are now rejected had no stored key; accepting them later (or changing parsing) may change what keys appear in progoptions. This is a compatibility consideration for indexing and resolution (locations: same as item 5 and get_objects which yields progoptions entries).
StepsToReproduce:
Create an rst file containing an option directive using a token with '[' and '='; for example:

.. option:: [enable=]PATTERN

   Immediately enable events matching PATTERN
Run Sphinx 3.2 to build the documentation (for example: sphinx-build -b html sourcedir builddir).

Observe the build log: a warning like "Malformed option description '[enable=]PATTERN', should look like ..." appears (and may be treated as an error). The option is not indexed or resolvable in the generated output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Sphinx accepts program option syntax strings that are valid for the documented program (including tokens containing '[' or '=' where appropriate), does not emit a "Malformed option description" warning for those tokens, and renders/indexes the option so it is visible and resolvable in the generated documentation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Sphinx 3.2 logs a "Malformed option description '[enable=]PATTERN' ..." warning for the option:: token and skips it. Because the token is not recorded in the standard domain's program-option index, the option is not resolvable or listed in produced indices. If all tokens in a signature are skipped, handle_signature raises ValueError, which can further cause the build to fail.

hints_text:
I disagree with 

> Sphinx ideally shouldn't change in ways that break the building of documentation that worked in older versions, because this makes it unworkably difficult to have documentation that builds with whatever the Linux distro's sphinx-build is.

The idea that things shouldn't change to avoid breaking is incredibly toxic developer culture. This is what pinned versions are for, additionally, you can have your project specify a minimum and maximum sphinx as a requirement.
I agree that there's some philosophical differences at play here. Our project wants to be able to build on a fairly wide range of supported and shipping distributions (we go for "the versions of major distros still supported by the distro vendor", roughly), and we follow the usual/traditional C project/Linux distro approach of "build with the versions of libraries, dependencies and tools shipped by the build platform" generally. At the moment that means we need our docs to build with Sphinx versions ranging from 1.6 through to 3.2, and the concept of a "pinned version" just doesn't exist in this ecosystem. Being able to build with the distro version of Sphinx is made much more awkward if the documentation markup language is not a well specified and stable target for documentation authors to aim at.

Incidentally, the current documentation of the option:: directive in https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/restructuredtext/domains.html?highlight=option#directive-option says nothing about this requirement for -, --, / or +.

For the moment I've dealt with this by rewriting the fragment of documentation to avoid the option directive. I don't want to get into an argument if the Sphinx project doesn't feel that strong backward-compatibility guarantees are a project goal, so I thought I'd just write up my suggestions/hopes for sphinx-build more generally for you to consider (or reject!) and leave it at that:

* Where directives/markup have a required syntax for their arguments, it would be useful if the documentation clearly and precisely described the syntax. That allows documentation authors to know whether they're using something as intended.
* Where possible, the initial implementation should start with tightly parsing that syntax and diagnosing errors. It's much easier to loosen restrictions or use a previously forbidden syntax for a new purpose if older implementations just rejected it rather than if they accepted it and did something different because they didn't parse it very strictly.
* Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

and on a more general note I would appreciate it if the project considered the needs of external non-Python projects that have adopted Sphinx as a documentation system but which don't necessarily have the same control over tooling versions that Python-ecosystem projects might. (The Linux kernel is another good example here.)

> Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

Major versions are done via semver, where Sphinx 2 is a major breaking change over Sphinx 1, and Sphinx 3 breaks changes over Sphinx 2. What other things could be done? The concept of deprecation isn't as common in Python communities due to the popularity of fixed versions or locking to a major version. IE ``pip install sphinx==3`` which installs the latest major sphinx version of 3.
This change was added at https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/pull/7770. It is not an expected change. It means this is a mere bug.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc treats explicit empty __all__ ([]) as if __all__ is absent
problem_statement.Description: When a module defines __all__ = [] (explicitly exporting nothing), autodoc's automodule :members: still documents all module members. The module-level explicit empty __all__ is being ignored.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The contract of sphinx.util.inspect.getall is that it returns None if a module has no __all__ attribute, and returns the __all__ Sequence (which may be empty, e.g. []) when __all__ is present. (sphinx/util/inspect.py::getall)

2) ModuleDocumenter.import_object stores the result of inspect.getall(self.object) on self.__all__. As a result, self.__all__ may be None (no __all__) or a Sequence (possibly empty) (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::ModuleDocumenter.import_object).

3) ModuleDocumenter.get_object_members uses a truthiness test ("if not self.__all__:") to detect the absence of __all__. This conflates two different states: None (no __all__) and an explicit empty sequence ([]). Because of that, an explicit __all__ = [] is treated like absence of __all__, causing the code path that yields all members to be taken (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::ModuleDocumenter.get_object_members).

4) ModuleDocumenter.sort_members uses a truthiness test ("if order == 'bysource' and self.__all__:") to decide whether to apply __all__-based ordering. This also conflates absence and an explicit empty __all__, causing ordering/selection logic to behave as if __all__ were absent when it is an explicit empty list (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::ModuleDocumenter.sort_members).

5) The mocking helper for autodoc (sphinx/ext/autodoc/mock.py::_MockModule) explicitly sets __all__ = [] for mocked modules. Treating an empty list as if it were absent would change the behavior for mocked modules and tests (sphinx/ext/autodoc/mock.py::_MockModule).

All of these findings share the same root pattern: code uses truthiness of self.__all__ to detect presence/absence of a module's __all__, but inspect.getall's contract distinguishes presence (returns a Sequence, possibly empty) from absence (returns None).
StepsToReproduce: Create example.py with contents: __all__ = [] and several functions (e.g. foo, bar, baz) each with docstrings.
Create an index.rst that uses .. automodule:: example with the :members: option.
Build the Sphinx documentation (e.g. run sphinx-build on the project).
Observe the automodule output for example.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Because the module explicitly sets __all__ = [], the automodule :members: listing should show no members (no entries for foo/bar/baz).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: All module members (foo, bar, baz) are shown under automodule :members: despite __all__ being an explicit empty list.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc fails to resolve struct.Struct type annotations (emits unqualified 'Struct')
problem_statement.Description: When a type annotation uses struct.Struct, Sphinx/autodoc emits an unqualified name "Struct" and fails to resolve it (warning: "class reference target not found: Struct"). The incorrect name is produced during annotation stringification/restification and is used as the cross-reference target, causing resolution to fail.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx.util.typing.stringify assumes that annotation.__module__ == 'builtins' implies the object is a real builtin and returns the object's __qualname__ (unqualified name). (Location: sphinx/util/typing.py, lines ~303–305: "elif (getattr(annotation, '__module__', None) == 'builtins' and hasattr(annotation, '__qualname__')): return annotation.__qualname__"). This produces "Struct" for a struct.Struct annotation when that module-string condition is true, rather than a dotted module-qualified name such as "struct.Struct".

2) sphinx.util.typing.restify uses a module-name-only test (cls.__module__ in ('__builtin__', 'builtins')) to decide to omit the module name and emit ':class:`%s`' % cls.__name__ (unqualified). (Location: sphinx/util/typing.py, lines ~89–100: "elif cls.__module__ in ('__builtin__', 'builtins'): return ':class:`%s`' % cls.__name__"). This same brittle assumption can produce unqualified class names for types that are not actually defined in the builtins module object.

3) sphinx.ext.inheritance_diagram.InheritanceGraph.class_name uses the same module-name-only check to drop module prefixes when module in ('__builtin__', 'builtins'), producing unqualified class names in diagrams. (Location: sphinx/ext/inheritance_diagram.py, lines ~221–231: "module = cls.__module__; if module in ('__builtin__', 'builtins'): fullname = cls.__name__ else: fullname = '%s.%s' % (module, cls.__qualname__)").

4) Downstream consequence in the python domain: sphinx.domains.python._parse_annotation and type_to_xref convert annotation text directly into pending_xref.reftarget (type_to_xref returns pending_xref with reftarget=text). (Locations: sphinx/domains/python.py, lines ~81–96 (type_to_xref) and ~158–165 (_parse_annotation): type_to_xref(... reftarget=text) and _parse_annotation replaces nodes.Text with type_to_xref results). Because stringify/restify/class_name can produce an unqualified textual name (e.g. "Struct"), that textual name is used as the cross-reference target; the resolver therefore looks up 'Struct' (not 'struct.Struct') and fails to resolve the reference, producing the observed warning.

Correct relationship (diagnostic specification): the logic that decides to omit the module prefix should only omit it when the object is actually a builtin as determined by membership in the builtins module object in addition to any module-name indicator. In other words, omitting the module prefix is only correct when the object's module string indicates a builtin (e.g. '__builtin__' or 'builtins') AND the object is present in the builtins module's namespace (for example, found in vars(builtins).values()). The current checks use only the module-name string and therefore misclassify some extension/foreign types whose __module__ value matches 'builtins' but are not entries of the builtins module object.
StepsToReproduce: Create docs/index.rst containing an automodule directive for your module (example shows helloworld.helloworld).

Create a module file (helloworld.py) with the following contents: import struct; import pathlib; def consume_struct(_: struct.Struct) -> None: pass; def make_struct() -> struct.Struct: mystruct = struct.Struct('HH'); return mystruct; def make_path() -> pathlib.Path: return pathlib.Path()
Run Sphinx build: python3 -m sphinx -b html docs/ doc-out -nvWT
Observe build warnings/errors about "class reference target not found: Struct" and that the struct.Struct annotations are not resolved/linked.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Annotations using struct.Struct are displayed with their module-qualified name (e.g. 'struct.Struct') and resolved/linked by autodoc in the generated documentation, analogous to how pathlib.Path is resolved.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Sphinx/autodoc emits the unqualified name "Struct" for struct.Struct annotations and fails to resolve it (build warning/error: "class reference target not found: Struct"). struct.Struct does not appear as a resolved/linked type in the output.

hints_text:
Unfortunately, the `struct.Struct` class does not have the correct module-info. So it is difficult to support.
```
Python 3.8.2 (default, Mar  2 2020, 00:44:41)
[Clang 11.0.0 (clang-1100.0.33.17)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'builtins'
```

Note: In python3.9, it returns the correct module-info. But it answers the internal module name: `_struct`.
```
Python 3.9.1 (default, Dec 18 2020, 00:18:40)
[Clang 11.0.3 (clang-1103.0.32.59)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'_struct'
```

So it would better to use `autodoc_type_aliases` to correct it forcedly.
```
# helloworld.py
from __future__ import annotations  # important!
from struct import Struct

def consume_struct(_: Struct) -> None:
    pass
```
```
# conf.py
autodoc_type_aliases = {
    'Struct': 'struct.Struct',
}
```

Then, it working fine.

problem_statement.Title: napoleon_use_param ignored for "Other parameters" section
problem_statement.Description: The "Other parameters" section is always formatted with the grouped/list style produced by _format_fields, ignoring the napoleon_use_param setting. This causes inconsistent rendering between the "Parameters" and "Other parameters" sections when napoleon_use_param is True.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py: GoogleDocstring._parse_other_parameters_section unconditionally calls _format_fields and does not consult self._config.napoleon_use_param. (See: def _parse_other_parameters_section(self, section: str) -> List[str]: return self._format_fields(_('Other Parameters'), self._consume_fields())) — this encodes the assumption that "Other parameters" must always use grouped/list formatting.

2) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py: GoogleDocstring._parse_parameters_section implements a different decision pattern: it checks self._config.napoleon_use_param and, when True, calls _consume_fields(multiple=True) and then _format_docutils_params. (See: if self._config.napoleon_use_param: fields = self._consume_fields(multiple=True); return self._format_docutils_params(fields) else: ... )

3) The parsing helpers that supply input for formatting already support the docutils-style emission used for parameters:
   - _consume_fields supports multiple=True and splits comma-separated names from the parsed _name string into separate (name, type, desc) tuples.
   - NumpyDocstring._consume_field parses NumPy "name : type" lines and NumpyDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs preserves comma-separated names (so _consume_fields(multiple=True) can produce separate parameter tuples).
   - _format_docutils_params constructs docutils-style ':param <name>:' and ':type <name>: <type>' lines.

4) The inconsistency is therefore encoded as a behavioral mismatch between _parse_other_parameters_section (unconditional grouped output) and the parameter/receives parsing methods (which honor napoleon_use_param). The code locations encoding these behaviors are the authoritative sources for this diagnosis.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL in an environment with Sphinx and napoleon available.
Run: from sphinx.ext.napoleon import NumpyDocstring

Run: print(str(NumpyDocstring("""
Parameters
----------
x : int

Other parameters
----------------
y: float
""")))
Observe the printed docstring rendering: the Parameters section uses :param/:type lines while the Other parameters section is rendered as a grouped header (e.g. ':Other Parameters: **y** (*float*)').
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When napoleon_use_param is True, the "Other parameters" section should be formatted like the "Parameters" section in user-visible output — i.e., emit docutils param roles such as ':param <name>:' and ':type <name>: <type>' for entries listed under "Other parameters".
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The "Other parameters" section is rendered with a grouped/list style header (e.g. ':Other Parameters: **y** (*float*)') regardless of napoleon_use_param, while the "Parameters" section follows napoleon_use_param and emits ':param' and ':type' lines.

problem_statement.Title: viewcode generates module pages for epub builds even when viewcode_enable_epub=False
problem_statement.Description: When building HTML and EPUB together (for example, running `make html epub`) the sphinx.ext.viewcode extension creates module pages for the EPUB output even though conf.py sets viewcode_enable_epub = False.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::collect_pages is registered on the 'html-collect-pages' event (setup connects it) and yields module pages unconditionally; collect_pages does not check the builder name (app.builder.name) or env.config.viewcode_enable_epub before producing pages. 2) sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::doctree_read already enforces the intended policy for doctree processing by returning early when app.builder.name.startswith("epub") and not env.config.viewcode_enable_epub; collect_pages does not mirror this decision, producing an inconsistency between doctree insertion of viewcode links and page generation. 3) sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py::gen_pages_from_extensions emits the 'html-collect-pages' event for StandaloneHTMLBuilder and its subclasses; because EpubBuilder inherits this behavior, event handlers that do not perform a per-builder/config check will be executed during epub builds. 4) sphinx/builders/_epub_base.py::EpubBuilder sets out_suffix = '.xhtml' (and link_suffix accordingly), and sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::should_generate_module_page and other viewcode code use builder.out_suffix and OUTPUT_DIRNAME ('_modules') to compute filenames; as a result, viewcode generation that runs during an epub build will create .xhtml module pages in the EPUB output. 5) sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::env_merge_info merges other._viewcode_modules into the main environment, so state about which modules have source available can be propagated across subprocess/environments; this makes the multi-step/multi-builder lifecycle (e.g. html then epub in the same run or combined run) relevant to whether pages are generated. 6) The root cause is an inconsistent per-builder gating policy: doctree insertion is gated for epub when viewcode_enable_epub is False, but page-generation (collect_pages) is not guarded the same way; the code locations involved are listed above and all contribute equally to the observed behavior.
StepsToReproduce: In conf.py set viewcode_enable_epub = False
Run: make html epub
Inspect the EPUB build output (and build staging directories) for generated module pages (for example, the _modules directory and generated .xhtml module pages)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When viewcode_enable_epub is False, the viewcode extension does not create module pages for EPUB builds; no module pages (no '_modules' output and no generated module .xhtml pages) should appear in the EPUB output.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Module pages (the '_modules' output and generated module pages) are produced for the EPUB build even though viewcode_enable_epub is set to False.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc: annotation-only inherited class attribute treated as undocumented
problem_statement.Description: When documenting a subclass with ``.. autoclass:: Bar`` plus ``:members:`` and ``:inherited-members:``, an annotation-only attribute declared in the superclass with an inline ``#:`` comment (PEP-526 style) is omitted (treated as undocumented). The attribute only appears if ``:undoc-members:`` is explicitly given.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py::get_class_members encodes the assumption that analyzer-provided attribute comments and annotation-only entries need only be added when a member name is not already in the local members mapping (expressed by membership tests such as `if name not in members`). As a result, when an earlier discovery path has already created a members[name] placeholder, get_class_members does not attach analyzer docstrings nor ensure the ObjectMember.class_ owner is set on that existing entry.

2) sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py::get_object_members contains the same pattern for analyzer-discovered attributes: analyzer contributions are appended only when `name not in members`. This produces the same failure-to-merge pattern for object-level discovery code paths.

3) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::Documenter.filter_members expects ObjectMember instances to carry analyzer docstrings and class ownership information in order to treat members as documented or to recognize their owning class. In particular, filter_members prefers an ObjectMember.docstring (it uses it to decide has_doc) and checks ObjectMember.class_ when deciding whether an inherited member belongs to a given superclass. If importer did not attach/merge analyzer docstrings and class_ into existing ObjectMember entries, filter_members can treat the member as undocumented or not belonging to the superclass.

4) sphinx/pycode (ModuleAnalyzer and parser) does collect inline attribute comments and exposes them via analyzer.attr_docs keyed by (scope, name) where scope corresponds to the containing class qualname (or '' for module-level attributes). The analyzer data exists and is available to autodoc, but the class-member discovery code path fails to attach that data to pre-existing ObjectMember entries.

5) For comparison, module-level member discovery (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::ModuleDocumenter.get_module_members) attaches analyzer.attr_docs to ObjectMember entries when creating them. That sibling code path demonstrates the intended pattern of associating analyzer docstrings with ObjectMember instances at creation; the class-member path differs in that it skips merging when an entry already exists.
StepsToReproduce:
Create example.py containing:

class Foo:
    """docstring"""
    attr1: int  #: docstring

class Bar(Foo):
    """docstring"""
    attr2: str  #: docstring

Create index.rst containing:

.. autoclass:: example.Bar
   :members:
   :inherited-members:
Run Sphinx (e.g. sphinx-build -b html . _build) and inspect the generated documentation for example.Bar
Observe that attr2 is documented but attr1 (inherited, annotation-only with inline comment) is not shown unless :undoc-members: is added to the autoclass directive
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The inherited annotation-only attribute (Foo.attr1 with inline "#: docstring") should appear in the generated documentation for Bar when using ``:members:`` and ``:inherited-members:`` (without requiring ``:undoc-members:``). The inline attribute comment should be shown as the attribute's documentation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The inherited annotation-only attribute is omitted from the subclass documentation (treated as undocumented). The attribute only appears if ``:undoc-members:`` is additionally specified.
